narrative:
Quillbrook Appraisal Partners is a five-person commercial real-estate
appraisal practice: one Managing Partner who signs everything a client
relies on, three appraisers who walk the properties, and an Office Manager
who keeps the books tighter than most firms twice the size. Clients are
lenders and property holders who need defensible valuations, so the
documents read accordingly: engagement letters where the commercial terms
are executed on the signature page rather than recited in the body,
kickoff memos that list the parcels in scope, working-session minutes kept
as undated field records (the file name carries the session date, the way
the firm's document management system requires), and status reports a
credit committee can file without edits. Tone is measured and precise;
numbers appear only where the record requires them, names appear in full
on anything with a signature line.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0001",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text. You do not know fact
  values; never write a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Portfolio Valuation for Perkins Group

authors.name: Keith Wilson
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Keith Wilson treats his signature as the firm's product and edits accordingly, reading every engagement letter and status report as though a lender's counsel will. He came up through fieldwork and still distrusts any conclusion he cannot trace back to a walked property or a documented comparable. In writing he is spare and formal, favoring short declarative sentences and striking any adjective that a credit committee might question.

participants.name: Joseph Fisher
participants.title: Senior Appraiser
participants.persona: Joseph Fisher is the appraiser the firm sends when a property or a client is difficult, and his files show it: dense field notes, methodical reconciliation, no loose ends. He writes the way he inspects, front-to-back and in order, and he is quietly impatient with prose that editorializes ahead of the evidence. Colleagues bring him drafts because his margin comments are blunt but always about the record, never the writer.

participants.name: Marcus Burnett
participants.title: Research Analyst
participants.persona: Marcus Burnett works the desk side of valuation, pulling comparables, verifying transaction details by phone, and building the market context the appraisers walk in with. He is the one who notices when a source contradicts itself, and he flags it in writing rather than smoothing it over. His memos are tabular by instinct, terse in prose, and scrupulous about distinguishing what was verified from what was merely reported.

participants.name: David Ellison
participants.title: Staff Appraiser
participants.persona: David Ellison is the newest of the fee appraisers and compensates with preparation, arriving at every site visit with a checklist he built himself and refuses to skip. His drafts run long because he documents everything he saw, and he relies on review to tell him what the file actually needs. His written voice is earnest and careful, closer to a field log than a letter, and he asks questions in memos rather than assuming.

docs.engagement_summary: Portfolio Valuation for Perkins Group, running about 6 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement: scope, approach, team, fee and start date, closing. End with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. 3-6 paragraphs. Commercial fee terms are executed on the signature page by counsel; do not state, estimate, or reference the fee amount anywhere in the text.

docs.guidance: Minutes of a working session: a list block naming every attendee (full names), discussion summary paragraphs, and a table or list of action items with owners. Do not state the meeting date anywhere in the text, in any format, and do not include a sigblock; this record is dated by its filename only.
